claims.claim_text: The module presents a supposedly real outage at a major tech company caused by a stray edit to `nginx.conf`, leading to site downtime and lost revenue.
claims.anchor_text: Consider a real-world incident at a major tech company: a junior engineer needed to update a simple setting in a load balancer. They opened the `nginx.conf` file in `nano`, accidentally typed a few stray characters while trying to save, and saved the file. Within minutes, the entire site went down, costing thousands of dollars in revenue. Why? Because they were editing directly on the production server without realizing it, and a single typo in a configuration file can break a service.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Consider a realistic production mistake: an engineer editing a load balancer configuration directly on a live server could save stray characters into `nginx.conf`, and a single typo in a configuration file can break a service.
claims.rationale: This is framed as a real incident but provides no company, date, or source, so it cannot be honestly cited as written.

claims.claim_text: Vim uses modes, and `i` is one of the commands that switches from Normal mode to Insert mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Meet nano: Your First Terminal Editor
claims.rationale: The official Vim docs describe Normal and Insert modes and list `i` among the commands that enter Insert mode.

claims.claim_text: A shebang like `#!/bin/bash` is used when a script is executed directly, while explicitly running `bash script.sh` does not consult the shebang.
claims.rationale: The referenced article directly explains shebang behavior for direct execution and notes that explicit interpreter invocation bypasses it.

claims.claim_text: `chmod +x` adds execute permission so a file can be run directly as a program.
claims.rationale: The `chmod` reference directly covers changing execute permissions on Unix-like systems.

claims.claim_text: The nano history fun fact includes a broken lineage through `pstrict` before correcting itself to `pico`.
claims.anchor_text: **nano is a clone of a clone.** It was created as a free replacement for an editor called `pstrict` which was a replacement for `pstrict` ... actually, nano is a free replacement for `pico`, which was the editor built into the Pine email client from 1989. The name `nano` derives from the SI prefix system, reflecting its role as a successor to pico (nano is 1,000 times larger than pico).
claims.suggested_rewrite: **nano is closely related to `pico`.** It was created as a free replacement for `pico`, and the name is a playful nod to the SI prefixes `pico` and `nano`.
claims.rationale: The `pstrict` wording is erroneous as written, so this sentence needs a factual rewrite rather than a citation pasted onto a broken claim.

claims.claim_text: The module says `ed` is the oldest surviving text editor still in use.
claims.anchor_text: **The oldest surviving text editor still in use is `ed`, created in 1969.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **One early Unix text editor still worth knowing about is `ed`.**
claims.rationale: This is a hard-to-prove superlative and should be rewritten to a defensible historical description.

claims.claim_text: `ed` is a line editor associated with early Unix development, and Ken Thompson developed it in 1969.
claims.rationale: The cited page directly identifies `ed` as a line editor and places Ken Thompson's work on it in 1969.

further_reading.title: Shebang (Unix)
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for why `#!/bin/bash` matters when scripts are run directly.

further_reading.title: Vim Reference Manual: Introduction
further_reading.why_relevant: Official Vim documentation for modes, insert commands, and the editor model contrasted with nano.

further_reading.title: GNU nano
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides concise background on nano's history, relationship to pico, and its control-key interface.

notes: Most of the module is pedagogical prose and command practice; the main citation-worthy items are the anonymous outage anecdote, specific editor-history claims, and shell execution mechanics.
